categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: The Habsburg kings of Spain — rulers of the first global empire

entries.label: Charles I (Charles V) — empire on which the sun never sets
entries.hint: Charles inherits Spain, the Americas, the Holy Roman Empire, the Netherlands and much of Italy simultaneously — the largest realm in Christendom since Charlemagne; his abdication in 1556 divides the empire between his son (Spain, Americas, Italy) and his brother (Austria, Holy Roman Empire).
entries.note: Election as Holy Roman Emperor 28 Jun 1519: Frankfurt electoral records. Abdication: ceremony at Brussels 25 Oct 1555 documented in eyewitness accounts (including by William of Orange). Simancas AGS Estado series: the primary archive for his entire reign. Brandi (1939), The Emperor Charles V: the standard biography. Simancas UNESCO inscription: 33 million documents covering his reign.

entries.label: Philip II — El Prudente and El Escorial
entries.hint: Philip II rules the first empire coordinated from a fixed capital — Madrid from 1561; builds El Escorial as palace, monastery and archive simultaneously; defeats the Ottomans at Lepanto (1571) but loses the Armada (1588); his reign defines the Spanish Golden Age at its peak.
entries.note: El Escorial building accounts: AGS, Obras y Bosques. Madrid as capital decree 1561: AGS. Philip II's personal correspondence: AGS Estado — he wrote in the margins of state papers himself, providing unique primary source insight. Parker (1998), The Grand Strategy of Philip II: uses the Simancas archive extensively. Death certificate 13 Sep 1598: parish records, El Escorial.

entries.label: Philip III -- the valimiento begins
entries.hint: Philip III delegates governance almost entirely to his valido, the Duke of Lerma -- the first systematic institutionalisation of favourite-rule in Spanish history; his reign signs the Twelve Years' Truce (1609) and orders the expulsion of the Moriscos (1609-1614); modern revisionist scholarship (Feros, Williams) views this not as 'decline' but as a structural adaptation -- delegation allowing the monarchy to manage an unprecedented administrative burden.
entries.note: Philip III's accession 13 Sep 1598: AGS Estado. Feros (2000), Kingship and Favoritism in the Spain of Philip III: the standard revisionist study of the valimiento as adaptation, not decline. Williams (2006), The Great Favourite: the standard English biography of Lerma. The Moriscos expulsion order (entry r2) and the Twelve Years' Truce (entry w4) both fall within this reign -- documented.

entries.label: Duke of Lerma -- the first valido
entries.hint: Francisco Gomez de Sandoval, Duke of Lerma, becomes Philip III's valido within days of his accession -- pioneering the systematic institutionalisation of favourite-rule that structures Spanish governance for the next century; he relocates the court to Valladolid (1601-1606), a move widely understood as real-estate speculation benefiting his own landholdings, before returning it to Madrid; his fall in 1618 (he retreats to a cardinalate to escape prosecution) sets the template his successor Olivares will follow.
entries.note: Lerma's appointment Sep 1598: AGS Estado. The Valladolid relocation 1601-1606: documented in municipal records of both cities (AHN, Consejos) -- Lerma's personal property speculation in Valladolid is documented in his own estate records. Feros (2000), Kingship and Favoritism in the Spain of Philip III: the standard study. Lerma's 1618 cardinalate (granted by Paul V specifically to grant him immunity from prosecution): documented in Vatican records -- an extraordinary case of ecclesiastical office used as legal shield.

entries.label: Olivares — the Union of Arms and the revolts of 1640
entries.hint: Gaspar de Guzmán, Count-Duke of Olivares, is Philip IV's chief minister — his Union of Arms (1626) attempts to spread the military burden across all Iberian kingdoms and the Italian territories; the resistance to this centralisation is the direct political trigger for the simultaneous revolts of Catalonia (7 June 1640) and Portugal (1 December 1640).
entries.note: Olivares's Union of Arms memorial (1624): AGS Estado — the primary policy document. The Catalan revolt 7 Jun 1640 (Corpus de Sang): documented in the Generalitat archives. The Portuguese revolt 1 Dec 1640: Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo. Olivares's fall 1643: documented in court records. Elliott (1986), The Count-Duke of Olivares: the standard biography — uses the AGS archive extensively. The 1640 revolts directly connected to Olivares: explicit in Elliott.

entries.label: Philip IV -- Olivares, the 1640 crisis, and Velazquez's court
entries.hint: Philip IV's 44-year reign is dominated first by Olivares (entry k4, until 1643) and his Union of Arms reform programme, which directly triggers the 1640 revolts in Catalonia and Portugal; the second half of the reign manages the resulting wars (Portuguese Restoration to 1668, Catalan Revolt to 1652, the Franco-Spanish War to 1659) under a succession of lesser ministers; Philip IV is also the great patron of Velazquez (entry a2), whose court portraits document the king's psychological transformation from confident youth to exhausted old age across four decades.
entries.note: Philip IV's accession 31 Mar 1621: AGS Estado. Olivares's fall 1643: documented in royal correspondence (AGS Estado). The 1640 revolts (entries c9, c10): direct consequence of Olivares's Union of Arms, documented in Elliott (1986), The Count-Duke of Olivares. Velazquez's sequence of royal portraits (Prado, multiple): the visual record of the reign's psychological arc -- documented in Brown (1986), Velazquez: Painter and Courtier.

entries.label: Charles II -- the end of the Habsburg line
entries.hint: Charles II, afflicted from birth by the cumulative effects of generations of Habsburg intermarriage, reigns for 35 years without producing an heir -- his court is consumed by factional struggles over the succession between French and Austrian Habsburg candidates; his will (2 October 1700), bequeathing the entire empire to Philip of Anjou, triggers the War of Spanish Succession documented in the following folio; revisionist scholarship (Storrs) emphasises that the empire he leaves, while diminished from its 1600 extent, remains a functioning composite monarchy, not a collapsed one.
entries.note: Charles II's will 2 Oct 1700: AGS Estado legajo 3001 -- the document triggering the War of Spanish Succession (documented in the following folio's wars lane). His medical history: documented in court physicians' reports (Archivo del Palacio Real) -- the genetic consequences of Habsburg consanguinity are now confirmed by modern genetic analysis (Alvarez et al. 2009, PLOS ONE). Storrs (2006), The Resilience of the Spanish Monarchy 1665-1700: the standard revisionist study arguing against 'collapse' framing for this reign specifically.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: European wars, Ottoman confrontation and colonial conquest

entries.label: Conquest of Mexico — Cortés and Tenochtitlan
entries.hint: Hernán Cortés with 500 men and indigenous allies destroys the Aztec empire — Tenochtitlan falls on 13 August 1521; the conquest of the most powerful state in the Americas is accomplished in two years, transforming the known world.
entries.note: Cortés's Cartas de Relación (5 letters to Charles V, 1519–1526): first published in Seville 1522–1525; BNE holds early editions. The Aztec account: Sahagún, Florentine Codex Book XII (Nahuatl-Spanish, 1577): BNAH Mexico City. Cortés's appointment: AGS. Restall (2003), Seven Myths of the Spanish Conquest: the standard modern reassessment using both Spanish and indigenous sources.

entries.label: Germanias -- the Revolt of the Brotherhoods (1519-1523)
entries.hint: Parallel to and simultaneous with the Castilian Comuneros revolt, the Germanias ('brotherhoods') in Valencia and Mallorca combine anti-seigneurial uprisings by urban guilds and peasants with forced conversions of Muslim vassals belonging to rebellious nobles; far more violent than the Comuneros in places, the revolt is crushed by 1523 with mass executions; omitting it from the standard Comuneros-centred narrative presents an overly Castilian-centric view of the crisis that opens Charles I's reign.
entries.note: Germanias records: Archivo del Reino de Valencia. The forced conversions of Muslim vassals: documented as a direct cause of later Morisco tensions -- Garcia Carcel (1981), Las Germanias de Valencia: the standard study. The simultaneity with the Comuneros (c6): both 1519-1523, documented as parallel but historiographically separated crises. The mass executions following suppression: documented in municipal records (Archivo Municipal de Valencia).

entries.label: Revolt of the Comuneros — the crushing of Castilian constitutionalism
entries.loc: Castile (Toledo; Segovia; Valladolid); Battle of Villalar
entries.hint: The Castilian Comunidades revolt against Charles I's foreign Flemish-dominated court and heavy imperial taxation — the Battle of Villalar (23 April 1521) crushes the Comuneros and permanently breaks urban constitutional resistance in Castile; royal absolutism is consolidated and the tax burden of Habsburg wars falls entirely on Castilian commoners.
entries.note: The Battle of Villalar 23 Apr 1521: AGS Guerra Antigua. The Comunero leaders (Padilla, Bravo, Maldonado) executed the following day: documented in contemporary accounts. The Comunero manifesto: published pamphlets — BNE. Haliczer (1981), The Comuneros of Castile: the standard English study. The long-term consequence: the Cortes of Castile never again successfully challenges royal fiscal demands — documented in subsequent Cortes proceedings (AHN Consejos). Charles I's response: documented in his correspondence (AGS Estado).

entries.label: Conquest of Peru — Pizarro and the Inca Empire
entries.hint: Francisco Pizarro captures the Inca emperor Atahualpa at Cajamarca (16 November 1532) — ransomed for a room full of gold and silver and then executed; within 40 years the largest empire in pre-Columbian America is reduced to a Spanish viceroyalty.
entries.note: Pizarro's appointment: AGS. The ransom room dimensions: Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva corónica y buen gobierno (c.1615) — Andean perspective, Royal Library of Denmark (GKS 2232 4°). Pedro Pizarro, Relación del descubrimiento (1571): eyewitness account. The capture at Cajamarca 16 Nov 1532: confirmed across Spanish and Andean sources.

entries.label: Duke of Alba and the Council of Troubles (1567-1573)
entries.hint: Fernando Alvarez de Toledo, Duke of Alba, arrives in the Low Countries in 1567 with 10,000 troops and establishes the Council of Troubles -- a special tribunal that tries over 12,000 people and executes more than 1,000, including the popular noblemen Egmont and Hoorne; Alba's repression becomes the central episode of the anti-Spanish 'Black Legend' propagated through Dutch and English pamphlet literature for centuries; rather than pacifying the Netherlands, his methods accelerate the revolt that becomes the Eighty Years' War (entry c7).
entries.note: Council of Troubles records: Algemeen Rijksarchief, Brussels -- over 12,000 cases documented. The executions of Egmont and Hoorne (5 Jun 1568): documented contemporary accounts, became iconic martyrdom images in Dutch propaganda. Alba's own correspondence with Philip II: AGS Estado -- documents his methods and the king's instructions. Parker (1977), The Dutch Revolt: the standard study of how Alba's repression backfired. The 'Black Legend' literature: Apologie of William of Orange (1581) explicitly uses Alba as its central villain -- BNE holds period editions.

entries.label: The Spanish Road -- Europe's logistical masterpiece
entries.loc: Milan to Flanders, via Franche-Comte and Lorraine
entries.hint: The Spanish Road (Camino Espanol) is the overland supply route by which Spain moves troops, silver and supplies from its Italian possessions (Milan) to the army fighting in Flanders (entry c7) -- carefully routed through friendly or neutral territory (Savoy, Franche-Comte, Lorraine) to avoid hostile France; the Duke of Alba's army marches this route in 1567 (entry c10) to crush the Dutch revolt, and the route remains in use until growing French control of the corridor and the 1633 death of the Duchess of Savoy force a shift to maritime supply via the Atlantic -- a logistical achievement enabling Spain to project military power across the length of Europe for over six decades.
entries.note: Alba's 1567 march (10,000 troops, Milan to Brussels in under six weeks): documented in Spanish and Franche-Comte municipal records of troop passage and provisioning. Parker (1972), The Army of Flanders and the Spanish Road 1567-1659: the standard and definitive study, using Spanish military pay and supply records (AGS, Guerra). The route's closure by the 1630s (French annexation of key passes): documented in the same Parker study. The connection to Rocroi (entry c13, 1643) and the broader Franco-Spanish War: the Spanish Road's closure is a direct precondition for the military difficulties culminating at Rocroi.

entries.label: Eighty Years' War — the Flanders swamp that bled Spain dry
entries.loc: Spanish Netherlands (mod. Belgium; Netherlands); Spanish Road
entries.hint: The Dutch Revolt (1568–1648) is the absolute military and economic centrepiece of the Habsburg era — the 'Flanders swamp' that consumes the American silver, drives the Philip II bankruptcies, requires the Army of Flanders, and forces the 'Spanish Road' logistics route from Milan to Brussels; it ends at Westphalia when Spain recognises the Dutch Republic.
entries.note: The Army of Flanders records: AGS Guerra Antigua — the most detailed surviving records of any early modern military force. The 'Spanish Road': Parker (1972), The Army of Flanders and the Spanish Road 1567–1659: the standard study. Alba's Council of Blood (1567): documented in the Dutch State Archives (Nationaal Archief, Den Haag). Parker (1977), The Dutch Revolt: the standard history. The financial drain: Drelichman & Voth (2014) document the correlation between Flanders expenditure and the Philip II bankruptcies.

entries.label: Rebellion of the Alpujarras (1568-1571)
entries.hint: The Pragmatic of 1567 -- banning Morisco dress, language, baths and music -- triggers a desperate two-year armed uprising in the Alpujarras mountains involving an estimated 60,000 Moriscos; suppressed by Philip II's forces (including Don John of Austria, victor of Lepanto three years later), the surviving population is forcibly dispersed across Castile in 1571; the rebellion is the direct prelude to the 1609 expulsion, demonstrating that the expulsion's roots lie four decades earlier in systematic state surveillance and cultural suppression, not a sudden 1609 decision.
entries.note: Pragmatic of 1567: AGS Estado -- the legal instrument banning Morisco cultural practices. The rebellion 1568-1571: AGS Guerra Antigua -- military campaign records including Don John of Austria's command. The 1571 forced dispersal: documented in resettlement records (AHN) -- Moriscos relocated across Castile in small groups to prevent reorganisation. Caro Baroja (1957), Los moriscos del reino de Granada: the standard study connecting this rebellion directly to the 1609 expulsion (entry r2) as cause and consequence.

entries.label: Battle of Lepanto — Ottoman advance halted
entries.hint: The Holy League (Spain, Venice, Papacy) destroys the Ottoman fleet at Lepanto — the largest naval battle since antiquity; Cervantes loses the use of his left hand fighting here; it is the first major Ottoman defeat and the symbolic high point of Habsburg military power.
entries.note: Philip II's orders to the fleet: AGS Estado. The battle: documented in multiple contemporary accounts. Cervantes's own testimony about his wound: prologue to Don Quixote Part I. Participating ships' rosters: AGS Marina. Braudel (1972), The Mediterranean, Vol.II: the most detailed modern account. The date 7 Oct 1571 confirmed across all sources.

entries.label: Spanish Armada — the great failure of 1588
entries.hint: Philip II sends 130 ships to invade England — the Armada is scattered by storms and English fireships; 63 ships and 15,000 men are lost; the myth of Spanish invincibility is broken and England's naval power established.
entries.note: Philip II's planning documents: AGS Guerra Antigua — the most detailed surviving record of a pre-modern naval expedition's logistics. The Armada's casualty list: compiled from AGS by Mattingly (1959), The Armada. Wreck archaeology: multiple sites off the Irish coast, excavated 1968–present (Ulster Museum, Belfast). Philip II's letter on the defeat: AGS Estado — 'I sent the fleet against men, not storms.'

entries.label: Twelve Years' Truce (1609) — de facto recognition of the Dutch Republic
entries.hint: The Twelve Years' Truce (1609) suspends the Eighty Years' War -- during this period the Dutch VOC (chartered 1602) expands aggressively in Asia at Portuguese expense. The Dutch West India Company (WIC), responsible for the later military campaigns and conquests in Brazil (Salvador da Bahia 1624, Pernambuco 1630), is not chartered until 1621 -- precisely when the Truce expires and war resumes; Brazil's Dutch conquest is therefore a consequence of the Truce's END, not a benefit Spain granted during it.
entries.note: Twelve Years' Truce 9 Apr 1609: Archives des Affaires Étrangères and Nationaal Archief (Den Haag). The Dutch VOC (Asia, during the Truce) and separately the WIC (chartered 1621, Brazil campaigns after the Truce's expiry)'s Asian expansion during the truce: documented in VOC (Asia, during the Truce) and separately the WIC (chartered 1621, Brazil campaigns after the Truce's expiry) records (Nationaal Archief). Israel (1982), The Dutch Republic and the Hispanic World 1606–1661: the standard study. The Spanish debate over the truce: documented in the Consejo de Estado minutes (AGS Estado) — Lerma vs. the hawks. The truce is simultaneously Spain's greatest diplomatic humiliation and a temporary necessity for fiscal recovery.

entries.label: Thirty Years War and Peace of Westphalia (1648)
entries.hint: Spain fights the Thirty Years War on behalf of the Catholic Habsburgs — at the Peace of Münster (January 1648, part of Westphalia) Spain formally recognises the independence of the Northern Netherlands (the Dutch Republic); the Spanish Netherlands (southern, roughly modern Belgium) remain under Spanish Habsburg control until Utrecht (1713).
entries.note: Peace of Westphalia: original text at Niedersächsisches Landesarchiv, Osnabrück; Spanish ratification in AGS. The Spanish financial exhaustion: documented in AGS Contadurías Generales (Drelichman & Voth 2014). Elliott (1963), Imperial Spain: the standard narrative of the decline. The treaty is the foundation document of modern international law.

entries.label: Catalan Revolt -- the Reapers' War (1640-1652)
entries.hint: Olivares's Union of Arms -- demanding Catalonia contribute troops and money on the same basis as Castile, violating its traditional fueros -- triggers open revolt in 1640; Barcelona's councillors briefly declare a republic under French protection before accepting Louis XIII as Count of Barcelona; the war becomes entangled with the broader Franco-Spanish War; Barcelona finally surrenders to Philip IV's forces in 1652, one of the greatest constitutional crises in Spanish history, testing whether the composite monarchy's regional privileges could survive wartime centralisation.
entries.note: The 1640 revolt and the killing of the viceroy (Conde de Santa Coloma, 7 Jun 1640): documented in Catalan and Castilian records (Arxiu de la Corona d'Arago; AGS Guerra). The brief Catalan Republic and French protection (1641): documented in the Pau de Corbeil treaty with France. Barcelona's surrender 13 Oct 1652: AGS Guerra. Elliott (1963), The Revolt of the Catalans: the definitive English-language study. The direct causal link to Olivares's Union of Arms (entry k6/k4): documented throughout Elliott's study.

entries.label: War of the Portuguese Restoration (1640-1668)
entries.hint: On 1 December 1640, Portuguese nobles proclaim the Duke of Braganza as King John IV, ending the 60-year Iberian Union (entry w5); a prolonged but low-intensity border war follows -- Spain, simultaneously fighting the Catalan Revolt and the Franco-Spanish War, cannot commit sufficient resources; the Battle of Montes Claros (1665) confirms Portuguese independence militarily, and the Treaty of Lisbon (1668) formally recognises it -- the permanent loss of Portugal and its global empire is among the greatest geopolitical losses in Spanish history.
entries.note: The 1640 Lisbon coup (1 Dec 1640): documented in Portuguese and Spanish records (Arquivo Nacional Torre do Tombo; AGS Estado). Battle of Montes Claros (17 Jun 1665): AGS Guerra -- decisive Portuguese victory. Treaty of Lisbon 13 Feb 1668: AHN Estado -- formal Spanish recognition of Portuguese independence. Valladares (1998), La rebelion de Portugal 1640-1668: the standard modern study. The connection to the Iberian Union's end (entry w5): direct -- 1580-1640 union, 1640-1668 war to formalise the separation.

entries.label: Battle of Rocroi (1643) -- the tercio myth ends
entries.hint: At Rocroi, the young Duc d'Enghien (later the Great Conde) destroys the Spanish Army of Flanders -- the Spanish tercios, for over a century considered Europe's finest infantry, are annihilated, with the Tercio Viejo de Cartagena fighting to near-total destruction rather than surrender; news of the defeat reaches Madrid days after Olivares's fall, and Rocroi becomes, in subsequent French and Spanish historiography alike, the symbolic end of Spanish military supremacy -- though Spain fights on for sixteen more years until the Peace of the Pyrenees (entry w3).
entries.note: Battle of Rocroi 19 May 1643: AGS Guerra -- Spanish casualty figures (c.7,000 of c.17,000 engaged) documented alongside French accounts. The Tercio Viejo de Cartagena's last stand: documented in both Spanish and French regimental histories -- its colours were reportedly captured only after the unit was annihilated. Quatrefages (1979), Los tercios espanoles: the standard study of the tercio system's decline. The symbolic 'end of an era' framing: explicitly constructed in 19th-century French historiography (Rocroi as the birth of French military glory under Louis XIV) -- a useful case of myth-making worth noting alongside the factual defeat.

entries.label: Treaty of the Pyrenees — the real transfer of hegemony to France
entries.loc: Isle of Pheasants (border island, Bidasoa river)
entries.hint: Spain's war with France continues for 11 years after Westphalia (1648) -- the Treaty of the Pyrenees (1659) ends the Franco-Spanish War (entry c13, the wars-lane span) with Spain ceding Roussillon (northern Catalonia) and Artois to Louis XIV; the Infanta Maria Teresa's marriage to Louis XIV (with a renounced but later contested inheritance claim) becomes the pretext for the War of Spanish Succession four decades later -- the treaty that ends one war plants the seed of the next folio's opening conflict.
entries.note: Treaty of the Pyrenees 7 Nov 1659: Archives des Affaires Étrangères (La Courneuve) and AGS Estado. The signing location (Isle of Pheasants, a condominium of France and Spain): documented. The cession of Roussillon: text in the treaty. The María Teresa marriage clause: Article 33 — her renunciation of Spanish succession rights (later contested by Louis XIV). Lynn (1999), The Wars of Louis XIV ch.2. The connection to the spain-1610-1715 folio: this treaty is also covered from the French perspective. The Maria Teresa marriage clause's later relevance: directly connects to the War of Spanish Succession (1701-1714, entry c1 in the next folio, spain-1700-1808) -- Philip V's claim derives from this 1659/1660 marriage.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Imperial administration, colonial law and the Inquisition's legal framework

entries.label: Edicto de Fe — the Inquisition's annual public summons
entries.hint: Every year the Inquisition reads the Edict of Faith publicly in churches — listing heresies and calling on the faithful to denounce neighbours; the edicts survive in AHN and document what the Inquisition considered heretical across the entire period.
entries.note: AHN, Inquisición series: the primary archive, holding 49,000 trial records. Homza (2006), The Spanish Inquisition 1478–1614: Anthology of Sources — makes key documents accessible. Henningsen (1980), The Witches' Advocate: statistical analysis of 44,000 cases. The AHN Inquisición archive is the richest surviving record of marginalised lives in early modern Europe.

entries.label: Laws of Burgos — first colonial legislation
entries.hint: The Laws of Burgos are the first European colonial legislation — a direct response to Bartolomé de las Casas's testimony about indigenous abuse; they regulate the treatment of indigenous peoples in the Americas, though enforcement is minimal.
entries.note: NOTE: The Laws of Burgos (1512) predate the Habsburg period (1516) — they belong to Ferdinand's regency and the Catholic Monarchs era. Included here as the earliest colonial legislation that the Habsburgs inherit. Text: Archivo General de Indias (AGI), Indiferente General 419, L.4. First published Burgos 1513. Las Casas's testimony that provoked them: documented in his Historia de las Indias. Simpson (1929), The Encomienda in New Spain: the standard study of the laws' implementation. The gap between the law's text and colonial reality is itself a primary source on the nature of empire.

entries.label: Consejo de Castilla and the Chancillerias -- the judicial backbone
entries.loc: Madrid (Consejo de Castilla); Valladolid and Granada (Chancillerias)
entries.hint: The Consejo de Castilla, the supreme administrative and judicial body for the Castilian kingdoms, sits alongside the two royal high courts (Chancillerias) at Valladolid and Granada -- this judicial bureaucracy is the actual institutional machinery through which royal justice is administered, local disputes arbitrated, and decrees enforced across the peninsula; while councils for the Indies and other territories handle their respective domains, the Consejo de Castilla and its Chancillerias represent the day-to-day legal experience of most Habsburg subjects.
entries.note: Consejo de Castilla records: AHN, Consejos -- one of the largest surviving administrative archives of early modern Europe. Chancilleria de Valladolid (founded as a permanent body 1494) and Chancilleria de Granada (1505): both archives extant and partially digitised (Archivo de la Real Chancilleria de Granada). Kagan (1981), Lawsuits and Litigants in Castile 1500-1700: the standard study using Chancilleria case records -- documents the everyday functioning of this judicial system.

entries.label: Council of the Indies and the Laws of the Indies (1524-1680)
entries.loc: Madrid (Council); applied across New Spain, Peru and other viceroyalties
entries.hint: The Consejo de Indias (founded 1524) is the supreme governing body for the Americas and the Philippines -- appointing viceroys (New Spain and Peru being the two great viceregal seats), reviewing colonial legislation, and hearing appeals; its accumulated decrees are codified in the Recopilacion de Leyes de los Reinos de las Indias (1680), perhaps the largest body of colonial legislation ever produced -- covering urban planning (the grid-plan town layouts found from California to Argentina), indigenous protection clauses descending from the New Laws (entry o2), and administrative procedure for the entire viceregal system.
entries.note: Consejo de Indias founding 1524: AGI, Indiferente General -- the council's own records, one of the richest administrative archives in the world. Recopilacion de 1680 (4 volumes): BNE and AGI -- extant, multiple modern critical editions. The grid-plan town ordinances (Ordenanzas de Descubrimiento, Nueva Poblacion y Pacificacion de las Indias, 1573, incorporated into the 1680 Recopilacion): documented as the template for hundreds of colonial town foundations -- Crouch, Garr & Mundigo (1982), Spanish City Planning in North America: the standard study. The viceroyalties of New Spain (1535) and Peru (1542): both founding decrees AGI -- administered under this Council's authority throughout.

entries.label: Archivo General de Simancas — world's first purpose-built archive
entries.hint: Philip II issues the world's first archive regulations on 24 August 1588 — the Simancas ordinances define the archivist's role, access procedures and document organisation; the archive holds 33 million documents and is the primary source for the entire Spanish empire.
entries.note: UNESCO Memory of the World (2002). Philip II's ordinances 24 Aug 1588: AGS, text published. The archive's 33 million documents in 80,000 bundles: current holdings. PARES (Portal de Archivos Españoles): digitised access. The Simancas archive is — after the Vatican — the most important archive for global 16th-19th century history (UNESCO).

entries.label: New Laws of 1542 — Las Casas's greatest victory
entries.hint: After Las Casas's persistent lobbying, Charles V issues the New Laws abolishing indigenous slavery and the encomienda system — they provoke a revolt by Spanish colonists in Peru and are partially repealed; but they establish the legal principle that indigenous people are free subjects of the Crown.
entries.note: Text: AGI, Indiferente General 423, L.20. Las Casas's Brevísima Relación (1542): written to accompany these laws — BNE first edition 1552. The Peruvian revolt against the laws: documented in AGI Peru. Hanke (1949), The Spanish Struggle for Justice: the standard study of Las Casas and the New Laws.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The Counter-Reformation, the Inquisition and the expulsion of the moriscos

entries.label: Society of Jesus -- Ignatius of Loyola and the Counter-Reformation
entries.loc: Rome (founded); global mission network from Spanish/Basque origins
entries.hint: Ignacio de Loyola, a Basque nobleman and former soldier, founds the Society of Jesus -- approved by Pope Paul III in 1540 -- which becomes the single most influential Catholic order of the early modern world; Jesuit colleges spread across the Habsburg empire and beyond, providing the educational backbone of Counter-Reformation Spain, while Jesuit missionaries (Xavier in Asia, missions across the Americas) extend Spanish-originated Catholicism globally; the order's later expulsion from Spain (1767, documented in the following folio) marks the dramatic reversal of this centuries-long relationship.
entries.note: Papal approval (Regimini militantis ecclesiae, 27 Sep 1540): Vatican Secret Archives -- the founding bull. Loyola's Autobiography and Spiritual Exercises: BNE, multiple period manuscripts. The global Jesuit college network: documented in the Society's own archives (Archivum Romanum Societatis Iesu) -- by 1700 over 800 colleges worldwide. O'Malley (1993), The First Jesuits: the standard modern study. The 1767 expulsion (documented in the spain-1700-1808 folio, entry r3): the dramatic later reversal of this relationship.

entries.label: Council of Trent — Spanish Counter-Reformation
entries.hint: Spain is the Council of Trent's most powerful sponsor and most zealous implementer — Spanish Jesuit theologians shape its decrees; the post-Tridentine Church gives Spain's religious culture its distinctive severity that persists for centuries.
entries.note: Trent Acts: Concilium Tridentinum (Görres-Gesellschaft edition). Spanish delegation: AGS Estado. The Jesuits founded by Ignatius Loyola (Spanish Basque, 1534): Loyola's Spiritual Exercises first printed 1548 — BNE. The Spanish Inquisition's role in implementing Trent: AHN Inquisición. O'Malley (2013), Trent: What Happened at the Council: the standard modern study.

entries.label: Teresa of Ávila and John of the Cross — mysticism as primary source
entries.hint: Teresa of Ávila's Interior Castle (1577) and John of the Cross's Dark Night of the Soul (c.1578) are primary sources for the interior spiritual life of Counter-Reformation Spain — both wrote under Inquisition scrutiny; Teresa was investigated twice.
entries.note: Teresa's Interior Castle autograph manuscript: Biblioteca del Real Monasterio de El Escorial. Interior Castle first published 1588 (Salamanca): BNE. Teresa's Inquisition interrogation records: AHN Inquisición. John of the Cross manuscripts: Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial. Both were canonised (1622, 1726) — their Inquisition files document the tension between mysticism and orthodoxy.

entries.label: Expulsion of the Moriscos — 1609–1614
entries.hint: Philip III orders the expulsion of the Moriscos (converted Muslims) — between 275,000 and 300,000 people forcibly expelled from Spain in five years; the Valencia expulsion decree of 9 April 1609 is the primary document.
entries.note: Expulsion decree for Valencia: AGS Estado legajo 2025. Philip III's letters confirming the decision: AGS. The numbers expelled: Domínguez Ortiz & Vincent (1978), Historia de los Moriscos — between 275,000 and 300,000. The economic impact on Valencia (loss of agricultural labour): documented in contemporary fiscal records. Carr (1982), Spain 1808–1975 for long-term consequences.

entries.label: Immaculate Conception controversy -- theology, art and diplomacy
entries.loc: Seville (Franciscan-Dominican dispute); Madrid (royal diplomacy); Rome (papal arbitration)
entries.hint: The doctrine of Mary's Immaculate Conception -- fervently defended by the Spanish Crown, the Franciscans and popular devotion, but theologically contested by the Dominicans -- becomes a major point of state diplomacy with Rome, with Philip IV personally lobbying the papacy for doctrinal recognition; the controversy is also the central theme of Golden Age devotional art, with Murillo (entry a8) painting the subject at least twenty times and Zurbaran producing the period's most austere versions; Alexander VII's 1661 bull, while not making the doctrine dogma (that waits until 1854), forbids public attack on it -- a partial Spanish diplomatic victory.
entries.note: Philip IV's diplomatic correspondence with Rome on this issue: AGS Estado, Roma legajos. Alexander VII's bull Sollicitudo omnium ecclesiarum (1661): Vatican Secret Archives. Murillo's Immaculate Conception series (Prado, Seville Cathedral and elsewhere, multiple versions 1650s-1680s): documented in Prado catalogue. Stratton (1994), The Immaculate Conception in Spanish Art: the standard art-historical study connecting the theological dispute to the visual record. The Franciscan-Dominican dispute: documented in university faculty records (Seville, Salamanca) -- the doctrinal positions were taught as opposing 'schools' for over a century.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: El Siglo de Oro — Velázquez, Cervantes, El Greco and the Golden Age

entries.label: Tomás Luis de Victoria — polyphony at the Counter-Reformation
entries.hint: Tomás Luis de Victoria is the greatest Spanish composer of the Renaissance — his Officium Defunctorum (1605), written for the death of Empress María, is the masterpiece of Counter-Reformation polyphony; printed by the Royal Printer in Madrid.
entries.note: Officium Defunctorum first edition: Madrid, 1605 (Royal Printer) — BNE and British Library. Victoria's appointment to the Descalzas Reales convent, Madrid: Archivo del Palacio Real. Stevenson (1961), Spanish Cathedral Music in the Golden Age: the standard study. The Officium's dedicatory letter to Philip III: documents Victoria's position at court.

entries.label: Lazarillo de Tormes — the picaresque and the view from below
entries.hint: Lazarillo de Tormes (1554, anonymous) is the founding text of the picaresque novel — narrated by a pauper's son who survives by serving a succession of masters each exposed as hypocrites; its anti-clerical satire was placed on the Index; it is the satirical, gritty counterweight to the idealised literature of the Renaissance, documenting poverty and social mobility from below.
entries.note: First edition: Burgos (also Alcalá and Antwerp, all 1554): BNE holds copies. Placed on the Index of Forbidden Books 1559: documented. An expurgated version published 1573. The anonymous authorship: still debated — Diego Hurtado de Mendoza and others proposed. Rico (1987), La novela picaresca española: the standard modern study. The picaresque genre it founds: Guzmán de Alfarache (1599), Quevedo's El Buscón (c.1626) — all documented. The picaresque tradition Lazarillo founds is expanded by Mateo Aleman's Guzman de Alfarache (1599, BNE) -- a longer, more moralising second-generation picaresque novel that was, in its own time, more widely read across Europe than Lazarillo itself, translated into French, English, Italian and German within two decades of publication.

entries.label: El Escorial — palace, monastery and archive
entries.loc: San Lorenzo de El Escorial, Madrid (extant, UNESCO)
entries.hint: Philip II builds El Escorial as the physical embodiment of Habsburg ideology — palace, monastery, library and royal tomb in one building; Juan de Herrera's austere classicism defines Spanish architecture; the library holds 40,000 manuscripts and printed books.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). Building accounts: AGS Obras y Bosques. Cornerstone laid 23 Apr 1563: documented. Juan de Herrera as architect: his drawings survive in AGS Mapas, Planos y Dibujos. The library (Biblioteca de El Escorial): 40,000 volumes, catalogue published 1601. Taylor (1967), Architecture and Magic: Considerations on the Idea of the Escorial: the standard analysis.

entries.label: El Greco in Toledo — Byzantine meets Mannerism
entries.loc: Toledo (extant works in situ and Prado)
entries.hint: Domenikos Theotokopoulos (El Greco) settles in Toledo in 1577 — his elongated figures and electric colours produce the most distinctive visual style of the Spanish Golden Age; the Burial of the Count of Orgaz (1586, Santo Tomé, Toledo) remains in situ.
entries.note: El Greco's contract for the Burial of the Count of Orgaz: Toledo Cathedral archive (1586) — full text survives. The painting: in situ, Santo Tomé, Toledo. His payment records: Toledo notarial archives. Álvarez Lopera (1999), El Greco: the standard modern catalogue. The contract's provisions (including the price dispute) document both the commission process and El Greco's status.

entries.label: Lope de Vega and Calderón — Golden Age theatre
entries.loc: Madrid (Corral de la Cruz, 1579; Corral del Príncipe, 1583 — both extant plans)
entries.hint: Lope de Vega (1,500+ plays; Arte nuevo de hacer comedias, 1609) revolutionises European drama with the comedia nueva mixing tragedy and comedy; Calderón de la Barca's La vida es sueño (1635) is the metaphysical masterpiece of the age; the corrales de comedias (open-air public theatres) are the primary social and architectural sources for early modern performance.
entries.note: Lope de Vega, Arte nuevo de hacer comedias (1609): BNE — the manifesto of the new drama. La vida es sueño first edition (1636): BNE. The Corral de la Cruz (1579) and Corral del Príncipe (1583): floor plans documented in Archivo de la Villa de Madrid. Surviving corral: Corral de Comedias de Almagro (1628) — extant, UNESCO tentative list; annual festival of classical theatre. Shergold (1967), A History of the Spanish Stage: the standard study. Lope's documented 1,500+ plays: the largest output of any playwright in Western literature.

entries.label: Polychrome wood sculpture -- Montanes, Fernandez and Holy Week
entries.hint: Juan Martinez Montanes in Seville and Gregorio Fernandez in Valladolid pioneer hyper-realistic polychrome wood sculpture -- life-sized Christs and Virgins with inset glass eyes, real hair and ivory teeth, painted in oil to mimic flesh and blood -- designed to be carried through the streets during Semana Santa processions and trigger direct empathetic devotion in crowds; Juan de Mesa's Cristo del Gran Poder (1620, Seville) remains the centrepiece of the city's Good Friday procession to this day, an unbroken four-century tradition.
entries.note: Montanes's Cristo de la Clemencia (1603, Seville Cathedral): extant, documented commission records (Archivo de la Catedral de Sevilla). Gregorio Fernandez's Cristo Yacente (1614, Valladolid, Museo Nacional de Escultura): extant. Juan de Mesa's Cristo del Gran Poder (1620): still processions annually in Seville's Madrugada (Good Friday), Hermandad del Gran Poder archive -- a living four-century tradition. Webster (1998), Art and Ritual in Golden-Age Spain: the standard English study connecting these sculptures to Holy Week devotional practice.

entries.label: Don Quixote — first modern novel
entries.loc: Madrid (published by Juan de la Cuesta)
entries.hint: Cervantes publishes Don Quixote Part I in January 1605 — it sells out within a week; Part II follows in 1615; the novel is immediately translated across Europe and is arguably the most influential work of prose fiction ever written.
entries.note: First edition Part I: Juan de la Cuesta, Madrid, Jan 1605. Only a handful of Part I first editions survive; one at the BNE. The pirated edition (Lisbon 1605) confirms the original's success. Part II first edition 1615: BNE. Cervantes's imprisonment records (he was twice jailed for debt): AGS. Rico (2004), critical edition: the standard modern text. The novel's connection to the Inquisition: Cervantes was examined by inquisitors (documented in AHN). Cervantes's own captivity in Algiers (1575-1580, five years as a slave after his galley was captured) directly informs the 'Captive's Tale' (Part I, chapters 39-41) -- his ransom petitions and the testimony of fellow captives survive in the Archivo Historico Nacional, making this one of the best-documented cases of a major author's own life entering his fiction.

entries.label: Murillo and Zurbaran -- the Sevillian school of devotional painting
entries.hint: Francisco de Zurbaran (active Seville from 1629) paints austere, sculptural monastic saints in stark light -- the visual equivalent of Counter-Reformation ascetic discipline; Bartolome Esteban Murillo (1617-1682) develops a softer, sweeter devotional register -- luminous Immaculate Conceptions and genre scenes of Seville's street children -- that becomes the most widely reproduced religious imagery of the Spanish Golden Age. Together they define Seville, not Madrid, as the era's other great painting capital, and both are central to the Immaculate Conception controversy (entry r5).
entries.note: Zurbaran's monastic series (Carthusian and Mercedarian commissions, 1620s-1630s): Museo de Bellas Artes de Sevilla and Museo del Prado, extant. Murillo's Immaculate Conceptions (multiple versions, 1660s-1670s): Prado and Seville Cathedral -- directly tied to the 1661 papal bull documented in r5. Murillo's beggar-boy genre scenes (Munich Alte Pinakothek, c.1650s): a distinct register from his religious work. Stratton-Pruitt (2002), Bartolome Esteban Murillo 1617-1682: the standard modern catalogue. Both painters' Seville guild membership: Archivo de la Catedral de Sevilla.

entries.label: Velázquez at Philip IV's court — Las Meninas
entries.hint: Diego Velázquez serves as Philip IV's court painter for 37 years — Las Meninas (1656) is arguably the most analysed painting in Western art history; his court appointment records survive, and the Prado holds the world's most important Velázquez collection.
entries.note: Velázquez's court appointment 6 Oct 1623: Archivo del Palacio Real. Las Meninas (1656): Museo del Prado (P001174) — inventory first documented in 1666 palace inventory. Palomino (1724), El Parnaso Español: the earliest substantial account of Velázquez's life and work, written within living memory. Brown (1986), Velázquez: the standard modern study.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Humanism, scholasticism, political theory and the debate on empire

entries.label: Francisco de Vitoria — founder of international law
entries.hint: Franciscus de Vitoria's Relectiones Theologicae (1539) argue that indigenous peoples have natural rights and that war against them requires just cause — the founding text of modern international law and the law of nations.
entries.note: Relectiones Theologicae: first published Lyons 1557; Salamanca University library holds the lecture notes (relectiones). Vitoria's lectures were taken down by students — the manuscripts are the primary source. Pagden & Lawrence (eds.) (1991), Political Writings: the standard English edition. Scott (1934), The Spanish Origin of International Law: the foundational study of Vitoria's influence.

entries.label: Las Casas vs Sepúlveda — the Valladolid debate (1550–1551)
entries.hint: The most important ethical debate in the history of European colonialism: Bartolomé de las Casas argues indigenous people have full human rights; Juan Ginés de Sepúlveda argues they are natural slaves; Charles V suspends the conquests while the debate proceeds.
entries.note: Las Casas's Apologética Historia (c.1551): manuscript BNE. Sepúlveda's Democrates Alter (refused publication by the Spanish universities): manuscript AHN. The debate's proceedings: partially documented in Las Casas, Aquí se contiene una disputa (1552) — BNE. Hanke (1974), All Mankind Is One: the standard study. The conquests' suspension: documented in AGS. This is the founding debate of international human rights law.

entries.label: School of Salamanca — the Quantity Theory of Money
entries.hint: Salamanca scholastics Martín de Azpilcueta (Navarrus) and Tomás de Mercado analyse the American silver flood and formulate the Quantity Theory of Money — linking money supply to inflation — decades before Jean Bodin or northern European thinkers; they also develop early purchasing power parity theory; these are the founding texts of monetary economics.
entries.note: Martín de Azpilcueta, Comentario resolutorio de cambios (1556): the first statement of the quantity theory of money — BNE first edition. Tomás de Mercado, Suma de tratos y contratos (1571): BNE. Grice-Hutchinson (1952), The School of Salamanca: Readings in Spanish Monetary Theory: the standard English study establishing the priority claim. Muñoz de Juana (1998), Scholastic Morality and the Birth of Economics: the standard modern study. The quantity theory formulation: 'the more money there is in a country, the more expensive commodities become' — Azpilcueta explicitly states this in the 1556 text.

entries.label: Francisco Suarez -- Spain's greatest philosopher
entries.hint: Francisco Suarez, a Jesuit (entry r4) trained at Salamanca and teaching at Coimbra, produces in De Legibus (1612) the most systematic early-modern theory of natural law and the law of nations -- arguing that political authority originates in the people and is only delegated to rulers, and that international law binds even sovereigns; his work is read closely by Grotius, cited by Leibniz, and his social-contract reasoning anticipates Locke -- making him arguably the most internationally influential Spanish thinker of the era, the direct successor to Vitoria (entry t2) in the law-of-nations tradition.
entries.note: De Legibus ac Deo Legislatore (1612): multiple extant editions, Coimbra. Defensio Fidei Catholicae (1613), written against James I of England's divine-right absolutism: BNE -- the text most directly engaging with Protestant monarchy. Grotius's explicit citations of Suarez in De Jure Belli ac Pacis (1625): documented, established in modern scholarship on the natural-law tradition. Pena (2007), La teoria de la ley en Francisco Suarez: the standard modern study of his influence on Grotius, Leibniz and the social-contract tradition.

entries.label: Juan de Mariana -- tyrannicide and the limits of royal power
entries.hint: Juan de Mariana, a Jesuit historian, argues in De Rege et Regis Institutione (1599) that a tyrannical king who violates the fundamental laws of the realm may legitimately be deposed -- and in extreme cases assassinated -- by his subjects; dedicated to the future Philip III, the book is publicly burned in Paris in 1610 after its perceived role in inspiring Henry IV's assassin, and Mariana is briefly imprisoned by the Spanish Inquisition for an unrelated fiscal tract; his tyrannicide doctrine sits within the same Spanish constitutionalist tradition as the French Monarchomachs and anticipates later Lockean resistance theory.
entries.note: De Rege et Regis Institutione (1599): BNE, multiple editions. The Paris burning (1610, after Ravaillac's assassination of Henry IV): documented in French parlement records -- the book was publicly condemned by the Parlement de Paris. Mariana's brief Inquisition imprisonment (1609, over De Ponderibus et Mensuris, a fiscal tract criticising currency debasement -- directly connected to entry s3): AHN, Inquisicion. Lewy (1960), Constitutionalism and Statecraft During the Golden Age of Spain: the standard English study situating Mariana alongside the French Monarchomachs (cross-reference: france-1494-1610 folio).

entries.label: Arbitristas -- Cellorigo and Moncada diagnose the Castilian disease
entries.hint: Martin Gonzalez de Cellorigo's Memorial de la politica necesaria (1600) and Sancho de Moncada's Restauracion politica de Espana (1619) are the foundational texts of the arbitrista tradition -- reformist memoranda diagnosing Spain's structural economic crisis: American silver (entry s1) floods in and out without enriching the country, domestic agriculture and manufacturing are neglected and undercut by cheap imports, and the tax burden falls on the productive poor while nobility and clergy are exempt; their proposed arbitrios (remedies) -- taxing the nobility, restricting bullion export, protecting domestic industry -- are largely ignored, but they represent the first systematic economic analysis of a 'resource curse' in European history.
entries.note: Cellorigo, Memorial de la politica necesaria y util restauracion (1600, Valladolid): BNE, the foundational arbitrista text -- 'Spain is a body composed of incongruous parts, ill-joined together.' Moncada, Restauracion politica de Espana (1619, Madrid): BNE. The connection to the Vellon crisis (s3) and Mesta/Tasa del Trigo (s4): the arbitristas explicitly diagnose both as symptoms of the same disorder. Elliott (1977), 'Self-Perception and Decline in Early Seventeenth-Century Spain', Past & Present: the standard modern study establishing the arbitristas as proto-economists, though Elliott himself cautions against reading their pessimism as objective decline (cross-reference: k3, k6, k7's revisionist framing).

entries.label: Baltasar Gracián — The Art of Worldly Wisdom
entries.hint: Baltasar Gracián's Oráculo Manual y Arte de Prudencia (1647) is 300 maxims of cynical Baroque pragmatism — how to navigate power, society and self-presentation; celebrated across Europe immediately; Schopenhauer translates it into German (1862) and calls it 'the best life manual ever written'; Nietzsche cites it as an influence.
entries.note: Oráculo Manual y Arte de Prudencia (1647): BNE first edition. Gracián published under a pseudonym (Lorenzo Gracián) due to Jesuit censure. Schopenhauer's German translation (1862): Handorakel und Kunst der Weltklugheit — he explicitly calls it the best practical guide ever written. Nietzsche's citations: documented in Beyond Good and Evil §283. Hidalgo-Serna (1993), Das ingeniöse Denken bei Baltasar Gracián: the standard modern study. Gracián's other works: El Criticón (1651–1657) — an allegorical novel influenced by Ibn Tufail.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Silver from the Americas, imperial debt and the price revolution

entries.label: Mesta, wool exports and the Tasa del Trigo -- the structural choice against grain
entries.loc: Castile (transhumance routes, Extremadura to the Cantabrian coast)
entries.hint: The Honrado Concejo de la Mesta -- the powerful guild of merino sheep owners -- holds royal-protected grazing rights (canadas) across Castile that override local agricultural land claims, while wool exports to Flanders generate reliable Crown revenue through customs duties; simultaneously the Tasa del Trigo, a price ceiling on wheat first imposed under Philip II to protect urban consumers, makes grain farming unprofitable for Castilian peasants. Together these two policies -- one favouring pastoral wool over arable land, the other capping the price of what arable land does produce -- drive land abandonment, rural depopulation and Spain's growing dependence on imported Baltic grain by the 1600s.
entries.note: Mesta privileges and canada routes: Archivo de la Mesta (now in AHN). Klein (1920), The Mesta: A Study in Spanish Economic History 1273-1836: the standard English study. Tasa del Trigo: first systematic price ceiling under Philip II, repeatedly adjusted -- AGS, Consejo de Hacienda. Baltic grain imports to Spain by the 1600s: documented in Casa de Contratacion and northern port customs records. Vassberg (1984), Land and Society in Golden Age Castile: the standard study connecting Mesta privilege and grain price controls as a single structural problem, directly anticipating the arbitristas' diagnosis (entry t7).

entries.label: Seville's boom and rural Castile's depopulation -- one redistribution
entries.hint: While the Mesta/Tasa del Trigo combination (entry s4) drains rural Castile of agricultural labour and the plague (entry s5) compounds rural depopulation, Seville's population roughly quadruples between 1500 and c.150,000+ by 1600 -- becoming the largest city in Spain and, briefly, one of the largest in Europe. This is not two separate stories but one: the empire's wealth and opportunity concentrate in a single port city while the agricultural interior empties -- a redistribution of population that the arbitristas (entry t7) identify as a structural symptom, decades before modern demographers had the tools to confirm it from parish records.
entries.note: Seville population growth (c.60,000 in 1500 to c.150,000+ by 1600): Archivo Municipal de Sevilla census records. Vassberg (1984), Land and Society in Golden Age Castile: documents the corresponding rural depopulation through parish and seigneurial records. Nadal (1984), La poblacion espanola: the standard population history connecting urban growth and rural decline as a single redistributive phenomenon. The arbitristas' contemporary diagnosis (Cellorigo 1600, entry t7) explicitly identifies rural-to-urban migration as a cause of agricultural decline -- a remarkably modern structural observation three centuries before urbanisation theory.

entries.label: Silver from Potosí — the engine of the world economy
entries.hint: The discovery of the Potosí silver mountain in 1545 transforms the world economy — Spain extracts an estimated 45,000 tonnes of silver over 150 years; it funds the Habsburg wars but triggers inflation across Europe (the 'Price Revolution') and ultimately flows to China via trade.
entries.note: Casa de Contratación records (Seville): AGI — the most detailed record of transatlantic trade volume. The Potosí mine: discovered 1 Apr 1545 (documented in AGI). Silver tonnage estimates: TePaske (2010), A New World of Gold and Silver: the definitive quantitative study using AGI records. Flynn & Giráldez (1995), American Silver and the Manila Galleons: documents the China connection. Potosí: UNESCO World Heritage Site (1987).

entries.label: Philip II's bankruptcies — and Philip III's
entries.hint: Philip II defaults on his debts three times despite receiving more silver than any monarch in history (1557, 1575, 1596); a fourth default in 1607 occurs under his son Philip III; the gaps between American silver revenue and imperial expenditure are documented in extraordinary detail in Simancas.
entries.note: AGS Contadurías Generales, Legajos 84–92: the primary archive for Philip II's finances. Drelichman & Voth (2014), Lending to the Borrower from Hell: Debt and Default in the Age of Philip II — uses the Simancas data for a quantitative analysis. The 1575 bankruptcy document: AGS Estado. Carande (1987), Carlos V y sus banqueros: the earlier standard study.

entries.label: The plague of 1596-1602 and 17th-century demographic collapse
entries.hint: A major plague epidemic enters Spain through the port of Santander in 1596 and spreads across Castile through 1602, killing an estimated 500,000-700,000 people -- roughly 10% of Castile's population -- in a population already strained by emigration to the Americas, military recruitment, and the agricultural crisis (entry s4); further regional outbreaks recur through the century (Seville 1649 loses perhaps half its population to plague), combining with poor harvests to produce sustained demographic stagnation that 17th-century arbitristas (entry t7) identify as both symptom and cause of broader decline.
entries.note: The 1596-1602 plague: parish burial records (libros de difuntos) across Castile show 3-4x normal mortality -- documented in regional archives (e.g. Archivo Diocesano de Valladolid). Perez Moreda (1980), Las crisis de mortalidad en la Espana interior, siglos XVI-XIX: the standard demographic study using parish records. The 1649 Seville plague: Archivo Municipal de Sevilla burial records document catastrophic mortality. Nadal (1984), La poblacion espanola: siglos XVI a XX: the standard population history. Connection to emigration and military recruitment as compounding factors: documented in Casa de Contratacion emigration registers and recruitment levy records (AGS, Guerra).

entries.label: Vellón crisis — copper debasement and monetary collapse
entries.hint: Philip III and IV debase the currency by minting pure copper vellón coins while hoarding silver — runaway domestic inflation follows, a collapse in trade, silver disappearing from internal circulation, and a destructive dual-monetary system where silver commands a premium over face value; the vellón crisis is the domestic economic face of Habsburg imperial overstretch.
entries.note: AGS, Contadurías Generales: the primary archive for Habsburg monetary policy. The vellón inflation: Hamilton (1934), American Treasure and the Price Revolution, ch.5 — quantifies the debasement. The silver premium (agio): documented in merchant correspondence (Archivo de Protocolos, Madrid). Ruiz Martín (1975), 'La banca en España hasta 1782', in El Banco de España: the standard monetary history. The dual monetary system: merchants kept accounts in two currencies simultaneously — documented in notarial records.

entries.label: The Padron Real and Spanish navigational science
entries.hint: The Casa de Contratacion (entry ex4) maintains the Padron Real -- a continuously updated master map incorporating data from every returning pilot, kept secret as a state asset; Pedro de Medina's Arte de Navegar (1545) and Martin Cortes's Breve compendio de la esfera (1551) systematise dead-reckoning navigation and are translated into English within decades -- Cortes's work becomes the standard English navigational manual (as 'The Arte of Navigation', 1561), meaning English navigators who later raid Spanish shipping learn their craft from Spanish textbooks.
entries.note: Padron Real: documented in Casa de Contratacion administrative records (AGI). Pedro de Medina, Arte de Navegar (1545, Valladolid): BNE. Martin Cortes, Breve compendio de la esfera (1551, Seville): BNE; English translation by Richard Eden (1561) used as the Royal Navy's standard text into the 17th century -- documented in English naval education records. Sandman (2007), 'Spanish Nautical Cartography and the Padron Real', in The History of Cartography vol.3: the standard modern study.

entries.label: Bastion fortifications -- defending the Caribbean against privateers
entries.loc: Havana; Cartagena de Indias; San Juan
entries.hint: Following Francis Drake's sack of Cartagena (1586) and repeated raids on Havana, Spain undertakes a century-long programme of bastion-style fortification across its Caribbean ports -- angled bastions designed to eliminate dead ground and concentrate cannon fire, engineered by Italian-trained military architects (Bautista Antonelli and successors); Havana's Morro Castle, Cartagena's walls and San Juan's El Morro become some of the most sophisticated fortifications in the Americas, and Cartagena's defences successfully repel a major British assault as late as 1741 (entry c4 in the next folio, War of Jenkins' Ear) -- a 16th-century engineering investment still paying off two centuries later.
entries.note: Bautista Antonelli's fortification designs for Havana and Cartagena (from 1586): AGI, Mapas y Planos -- extensive surviving plans. Morro Castle, Havana (begun 1589): extant, UNESCO World Heritage. Cartagena's walls (extant, UNESCO World Heritage): construction records AGI. Marchena Fernandez (1992), Ejercito y milicias en el mundo colonial americano: the standard study of colonial military engineering. The direct line to Cartagena's 1741 defence (cross-reference: spain-1700-1808, entry c4): the fortifications built under THIS folio's timeframe are what Blas de Lezo defends.

entries.label: The galleon -- Iberian innovation in oceanic ship design
entries.hint: The galleon -- combining the carrack's cargo capacity with the war galley's lower, more manoeuvrable hull and reduced top-heavy superstructure -- emerges from Iberian shipyards by the mid-16th century as the dominant vessel for both transatlantic treasure transport and naval warfare; its design balances cargo space (essential for the silver fleets, entry s1) against the need to carry heavy cannon and survive Atlantic crossings, remaining the basic template for oceanic shipping for over two centuries -- the Manila Galleon (entry ex1) and the Spanish Armada (entry c2) are both built on this single underlying design innovation.
entries.note: Galleon design evolution from carrack and galley hybrid forms: Phillips (1986), Six Galleons for the King of Spain: Imperial Defense in the Early Seventeenth Century -- the standard English study using Spanish shipbuilding contracts (AGS, Marina). Basque shipyards (Pasaia, Zumaia) as major construction centres: documented in royal shipbuilding contracts. The design's dual use for treasure transport and warfare (the same hull type underlying both ex1 and c2): explicitly discussed in Phillips and in Casado Soto (1988), Los barcos espanoles del siglo XVI.

entries.label: The patio process -- mercury amalgamation and the silver economy
entries.loc: Pachuca, New Spain (invented); Potosi (scaled up from 1570s)
entries.hint: Bartolome de Medina develops the patio process in Pachuca in 1554 -- mixing crushed silver ore with mercury, salt and copper sulphate, spread on open patios and trodden by mules to amalgamate silver from low-grade ore that earlier smelting methods could not profitably process; the process is scaled up at Potosi from the 1570s and becomes the technological foundation of the silver flood documented in entry s1, while creating a permanent demand for mercury supplied from the Crown mercury mine at Huancavelica -- linking two mining sites a thousand miles apart into a single industrial system.
entries.note: Bartolome de Medina's 1554 process: documented in colonial mining records (Archivo General de la Nacion, Mexico). The Huancavelica mercury mine as the supply source for Potosi's amalgamation: Bakewell (1984), Miners of the Red Mountain: the standard study of the Potosi-Huancavelica industrial linkage. The patio process's productivity gains over earlier smelting (allowing profitable extraction from ore previously discarded as waste): TePaske (1983), 'New World Silver, Castile and the Philippines 1590-1800'. This technological innovation is the direct mechanism behind the silver volumes documented in entry s1 -- without amalgamation, Potosi's output would have been a fraction of its actual scale.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: The Manila Galleon, the Columbian Exchange and global Spain

entries.label: The Columbian Exchange — biological globalisation
entries.hint: The exchange of plants, animals and diseases between the Old and New Worlds is the most consequential biological event since the last Ice Age — tomatoes, potatoes, maize, chocolate go east; horses, cattle, smallpox go west; documented in Spanish colonial records.
entries.note: The exchange's documentary evidence: AGI and AGS — colonial reports describing new plants and animals (e.g. the potato's first European description by Cieza de León, 1553). Crosby (1972), The Columbian Exchange: the foundational modern study. The smallpox epidemic that preceded Cortés: documented in the Florentine Codex (Sahagún). The potato: Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (1553) — first European written description.

entries.label: Treaty of Tordesillas — dividing the world
entries.hint: The Treaty of Tordesillas (7 June 1494) divides the non-European world between Spain and Portugal along a meridian -- though signed before this folio's start, it remains the operative legal framework for the entire Habsburg period, repeatedly invoked in colonial boundary disputes (Brazil, the Philippines, the Moluccas) until finally superseded by the Treaty of Madrid (1750, documented in the following folio) which formally abandons exclusive Iberian division of the globe.
entries.note: Treaty of Tordesillas 7 Jun 1494: AGI (Patronato) -- the original document. Its continued legal invocation throughout the Habsburg period: documented in 17th-century boundary disputes (Brazil-Spanish America frontier negotiations, AGI Buenos Aires legajos). The Treaty of Madrid 1750 (entry ex7 in the 1700-1808 folio) as the formal end of Tordesillas-era exclusivity: direct cross-folio reference. Parry (1966), The Spanish Seaborne Empire, ch.1: the standard study of Tordesillas's continued legal force.

entries.label: Casa de la Contratación — monopoly, corruption and contraband
entries.loc: Seville (Casa de la Contratación); Cádiz (monopoly transferred 1717)
entries.hint: The Casa de la Contratación holds a strict single-port monopoly on all American trade — this rigidity fosters immense corruption (documented in its own records), chokes mercantile flexibility, and opens the American coasts to massive Dutch and English contraband trade; the monopoly is the institutional reason why Spain extracts less value from its empire than its rivals extract illegally.
entries.note: Casa de la Contratación records: AGI, Indiferente General — the primary archive. Founded 20 Jan 1503: AGI. The contraband problem: documented in AGI reports from colonial governors throughout the 17th century. Haring (1918), Trade and Navigation between Spain and the Indies: the standard study. The monopoly's transfer to Cádiz 1717: documented. The 1778 Free Trade Decree (see Bourbon Spain folio): the eventual dissolution of the Cádiz monopoly — a direct response to the failures documented here.

entries.label: Seville and the Consulado de Cargadores a Indias
entries.hint: The Consulado de Cargadores a Indias, founded 1543, is the merchant guild that controls the transatlantic fleets (flotas y galeones) -- financing royal loans (juros) in exchange for trade privileges and operating as a cartel that dictates which goods, in what quantity, travel to the Indies each year; this cartel structure is the commercial complement to the regulatory Casa de Contratacion (entry ex4) -- between them, a single port (Seville, later Cadiz) and a single merchant guild control the entire legal flow of goods between Spain and its American empire for over two centuries.
entries.note: Consulado founding 1543: Archivo General de Indias, Consulados. Garcia-Baquero Gonzalez (1992), La Carrera de Indias: the standard study of the Consulado's commercial dominance and its cartel pricing of the annual fleets. The Consulado's role financing royal loans (juros) in exchange for trade privileges: AGI, Indiferente General -- direct mechanism connecting merchant capital to the bankruptcies documented in entry s2. The Casa de Contratacion / Consulado pairing as regulatory/commercial halves of one monopoly system: Garcia-Baquero Gonzalez's central argument.

entries.label: Manila Galleon — the first Pacific trade route
entries.hint: The Manila Galleon establishes the first regular trans-Pacific trade route in 1565 — Mexican silver goes west to China; Chinese silk and porcelain go east to the Americas and Europe; a global trading circuit for 250 years.
entries.note: AGI, Philippines series: the primary archive for Manila Galleon records. Andrade (1519): the first voyage by Urdaneta, documented. Schurz (1939), The Manila Galleon: the foundational study. Flynn & Giráldez (1995) on the silver-silk exchange. Chinese porcelain from Manila Galleons recovered archaeologically in Mexico: multiple museum collections. The route operated 1565–1815 — 250 years of continuous trans-Pacific trade.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Habsburg empire in its global context

entries.label: The empire on which the sun never sets
entries.loc: Spain; Americas; Philippines; Italy; Netherlands; Holy Roman Empire
entries.hint: At its peak under Philip II, the Spanish empire encompasses the Americas, the Philippines, Portugal (1580–1640), southern Italy, the Netherlands, and parts of North Africa — the first genuinely global empire, administered from Madrid via a postal network that spans the world.
entries.note: The phrase 'on which the sun never sets': documented first in a 1557 document from the court of Charles V. The imperial postal network: documented in AGS. Parker (1998), The Grand Strategy of Philip II: maps and analyses the empire's logistical system. The Philippines acquisition 1565: AGI. The Portuguese union 1580: AGS Estado.

entries.label: The Iberian Union (1580-1640) -- the largest empire in history
entries.hint: Philip II's annexation of the Portuguese crown in 1580 (after the Portuguese succession crisis following King Sebastian's death at Alcacer Quibir) creates a single monarch ruling the largest joint empire in history -- the Spanish Americas and Philippines united with Portugal's networks across Brazil, Angola, Mozambique, Goa and Macau; this consolidation makes Spain's enemies (the Dutch and English) automatically the enemies of Portugal's empire too, directly triggering Dutch attacks on Portuguese Asia and Brazil (entry w4) that would not otherwise have occurred; the union ends with the Portuguese Restoration (entry c12) in 1640, after which Portugal's overseas empire separates permanently from Spain's.
entries.note: The 1580 annexation following the Battle of Alcantara: documented in both Spanish and Portuguese chronicles of the period. The Cortes of Tomar (1581), where Philip II swears to respect Portuguese institutional autonomy (a separate Council of Portugal, separate laws): AHN -- the legal basis of the 'union' as composite rather than incorporative. Boxer (1969), The Portuguese Seaborne Empire 1415-1825: the standard English study of how the union exposed Portuguese Asia and Brazil to Dutch attack (directly connecting to entry w4's VOC/WIC timeline). The union's end with the Restoration (1640, entry c12): the Duke of Braganza's acclamation as John IV explicitly invokes the illegality of the 1580 succession.

entries.label: The General Crisis of the Seventeenth Century
entries.hint: The mid-17th-century convulsions documented in this folio -- the Catalan Revolt, the Portuguese Restoration, Rocroi -- are not uniquely Spanish failures but Spain's local instance of a wave of simultaneous crises across Eurasia: the Fronde in France (1648-1653), the English Civil War and regicide (1642-1649), the Ming-Qing dynastic transition in China (1644), and unrest across the Ottoman Empire all cluster in the same two decades; modern climate-historical scholarship links this clustering to the 'Little Ice Age's coldest phase, producing harvest failures worldwide. Read this way, Spain's 1640s are not evidence of unique decline but of a genuinely global structural crisis that the composite monarchy, unusually, survives intact as a unified state -- unlike Ming China or the British monarchy, both of which fall.
entries.note: Parker (2013), Global Crisis: War, Climate Change and Catastrophe in the Seventeenth Century: the standard modern comparative study, explicitly using Spain alongside China, England and the Ottoman Empire as parallel case studies. Trevor-Roper (1959), 'The General Crisis of the 17th Century', Past & Present: the originating historiographical debate. The Little Ice Age's coldest decades (1640s-1690s, the 'Maunder Minimum' approaching): documented in tree-ring and ice-core proxy records. The comparative framing directly supports the revisionist reading of k6/k7 (Storrs, Elliott) -- Spain's survival as a unified composite monarchy through this global crisis, when contemporaries like the Stuart monarchy and Ming China did not survive, is itself the more remarkable story.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Black confraternities of Seville -- enslaved and free Africans' religious associations
entries.hint: The Hermandad de Nuestra Senora de los Angeles (popularly the 'Hermandad de los Negritos'), founded in the 14th century and continuously active through the Habsburg period, is a religious confraternity organised by and for Seville's enslaved and free Black population -- providing burial funds, hospital care and a recognised collective religious identity within the city's intensely hierarchical confraternity system; Seville's Black population (both enslaved domestic servants, cross-referenced with entry lv5, and a free community) numbers in the thousands during this period, making these confraternities a rare institutional space of Afro-Spanish collective life with continuous documentation across two centuries.
entries.note: Hermandad de los Negritos: archive held by the confraternity itself (Hospital de Nuestra Senora de los Angeles, Seville) -- one of the longest continuously-documented Afro-European religious institutions. Pike (1967), 'Sevillian Society in the Sixteenth Century: Slaves and Freedmen', Hispanic American Historical Review: the standard study of Seville's Black population using notarial and parish records. Fra Molinero (1995), La imagen de los negros en el teatro del Siglo de Oro: contextualises Black religious life within wider Golden Age society. The confraternity's continuous activity 1516-1700: documented through its own surviving administrative records spanning the period.

entries.label: Conversos under the Inquisition — blood purity statutes
entries.hint: The Estatutos de Limpieza de Sangre (blood purity statutes) from 1449 onwards bar conversos (Jewish converts) from public office, guilds and religious orders — the first racial discrimination laws in European history, a CATEGORICAL SHIFT from religious to ethnic persecution.
entries.note: Toledo statute 1449: the first limpieza de sangre statute — AHN. The Inquisition's genealogical investigations: AHN Inquisición — families tracked across generations for Jewish ancestry. Netanyahu (1995), The Origins of the Inquisition in Fifteenth Century Spain: the foundational modern study. The paradox: Cervantes and Velázquez were both likely of converso descent (documented by genealogical research). This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT from religious heresy to ethnic origin as the basis of persecution.

entries.label: Inquisition trials for sodomy — the systematic record
entries.loc: Inquisition tribunals across Spain and empire
entries.hint: The Spanish Inquisition tried hundreds of cases of sodomy — the AHN Inquisición archive contains the most detailed surviving early modern legal documentation of same-sex acts, including testimony, sentencing and punishment; the records allow reconstruction of lived experience behind the legal framework.
entries.note: AHN Inquisición: sodomy cases catalogued by Berco (2007), Sexual Hierarchies, Public Status: Men, Sodomy, and Society in Spain's Golden Age — the primary modern study using AHN records. Garza Carvajal (2003), Butterflies Will Burn: Prosecuting Sodomites in Early Modern Spain and Mexico. The records document both the acts and the social networks around them. The Inquisition's jurisdiction over sodomy (shared with secular courts): documented in the Instrucciones (1561).

entries.label: Urban pícaros and domestic slaves in Habsburg Seville
entries.hint: The American silver influx triggers massive inflation (the Price Revolution) destroying domestic industries — Seville and Madrid fill with urban poor, beggars and abandoned children; simultaneously, enslaved Black Africans and Morisco war captives constitute up to 10% of Seville's urban population, serving in households, shipyards and municipal services; the picaresque literature documents both realities.
entries.note: Seville's enslaved population (up to 10%): documented in parish records and notarial archives (Archivo Histórico Provincial de Sevilla) — Pike (1967), 'Sevillian Society in the Sixteenth Century', Hispanic American Historical Review: the standard study. The Price Revolution in Spain: Hamilton (1934), American Treasure and the Price Revolution in Spain: uses AGS data. The pícaros: contemporaries (Mateo Alemán, Quevedo) document them. Domínguez Ortiz (1955), La sociedad española en el siglo XVII: the standard social history. The Lazarillo de Tormes entry (a6) documents this reality from within.

entries.label: Moriscos under the Pragmatic of 1567 -- daily life before expulsion
entries.hint: The Pragmatic of 1567 bans the Moriscos (converted Muslims) of Granada from speaking Arabic, wearing traditional dress, using Arabic names, or maintaining their bathhouses and music -- an attempt at total cultural erasure enforced through systematic surveillance, denunciation and Inquisition prosecution; the desperation this produces erupts in the Rebellion of the Alpujarras (entry c9, 1568-1571), after which Granada's Moriscos are forcibly dispersed across Castile; four decades of this lived surveillance precede and explain the final expulsion (entry r2, 1609-1614) -- the 1609 decision is the culmination of a half-century of escalating cultural prohibition, not a sudden act.
entries.note: The Pragmatic of 1567 (Pragmatica de 1567): AHN, Inquisicion -- the text specifically prohibits Arabic language, dress, names, baths and 'zambras' (Moorish music and dance). Inquisition denunciation records for Morisco cultural practices: AHN, Inquisicion de Granada -- thousands of individual cases survive. The post-Alpujarras dispersal (1571, c.80,000 Moriscos relocated from Granada to Castilian towns): documented in royal resettlement orders, AGS. Garcia-Arenal (2009), Inquisicion y moriscos: the standard study using the Inquisition archive to reconstruct daily Morisco life under surveillance. Direct causal chain to entry r2: the 1609 expulsion decree's preamble explicitly cites the 'failure' of the 1567-1571 assimilation programme.

entries.label: Florentine Codex — indigenous voice in the conquest
entries.hint: Bernardino de Sahagún compiles the Florentine Codex with Nahuatl-speaking informants — a 12-book encyclopedia of Aztec life, including an eyewitness Nahuatl account of the conquest (Book XII); the most important indigenous primary source for the colonial encounter.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Florentine Codex (Códice Florentino): Biblioteca Medicea Laurenziana, Florence (Ms. Mediceo Palatino 218–220). Completed 1575–1577. Sahagún, Historia General de las Cosas de Nueva España: the same work in different manuscript form. Anderson & Dibble translation (1950–1982), 13 vols: the standard English edition. Book XII: the indigenous account of the conquest in Nahuatl — the most important non-Spanish primary source for 16th-century Mexico.

entries.label: Catalina de Erauso -- the Lieutenant Nun
entries.loc: San Sebastian (born); Peru and Chile (military career)
entries.hint: Catalina de Erauso escapes a Basque convent at fifteen, cuts her hair, and spends three decades in the Americas living as a man -- 'Alonso Diaz Ramirez de Guzman' -- serving as a soldier in Chile and Peru, duelling, gambling and rising to the rank of alferez (lieutenant) before a deathbed confession to a bishop in 1620 reveals her sex; rather than punishment, Pope Urban VIII grants her permission to continue dressing as a man (1626) in recognition of her unbroken virginity, and Philip IV grants her a military pension -- an extraordinary case of institutional accommodation for a gender-nonconforming life, documented in her own dictated memoir.
entries.note: Vida i sucesos de la Monja Alferez (dictated memoir, c.1626): multiple manuscript copies survive, BNE holds one. The 1626 papal permission to continue male dress: documented in Vatican records and contemporaneous Spanish accounts of her visit to Rome. Philip IV's pension grant: AGS, Guerra -- military pension records. Velasco (2000), The Lieutenant Nun: Transgenderism, Writing, and the Self in Colonial Spanish America: the standard modern critical study. Stepto & Stepto's English translation (1996) of the Vida: makes the primary source widely accessible.

entries.label: Maria de Zayas -- Desenganos amorosos and violence against women
entries.hint: Maria de Zayas's Novelas amorosas y ejemplares (1637) and Desenganos amorosos (1647) are framed story-collections in which women narrators tell tales of seduction, betrayal and violence by men -- the second collection is explicitly framed as a corrective 'undeception' aimed at warning women against marriage's dangers; Zayas was a literary celebrity in her lifetime, praised by Lope de Vega, but her unflinching depiction of domestic violence and her narrators' open distrust of men made her work controversial and led to her relative erasure from the canon until 20th-century feminist recovery.
entries.note: Novelas amorosas y ejemplares (1637) and Desenganos amorosos (1647): both BNE, multiple early editions attesting to commercial success. Lope de Vega's praise of Zayas in Laurel de Apolo (1630): documented, predates her major works -- she was known as a poet first. Greer (2000), Maria de Zayas Tells Baroque Tales of Love and the Cruelty of Men: the standard modern critical edition and study. The 20th-century recovery: documented in the publication history of modern critical editions, beginning in the 1970s-1980s after near-total neglect following the 17th century.

entries.label: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz — poet and proto-feminist
entries.hint: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz, a nun in colonial Mexico, is the greatest poet of the Spanish Golden Age — her Respuesta a Sor Filotea (1691) is the first feminist manifesto in the Americas, defending women's right to education against her bishop's censure.
entries.note: Respuesta a Sor Filotea de la Cruz (1691): manuscript at New York Public Library (Berg Collection). First published 1700 (Fama y Obras Póstumas). Sor Juana's convent records: Archivo del Antiguo Convento de San Jerónimo, Mexico City. Paz (1982), Sor Juana or, the Traps of Faith: the standard modern biography. Her forced 'renunciation' of learning (1693): documented in the convent's own records — the bishop's pressure and her submission.
